problems.title: A  function that is discontinuous at every real number
problems.problem: Find an example of a function that is discontinuous at every real number.
problems.hint: • Consider the function which takes a constant value on rationals and constant value on irrationals.
problems.solution:
Consider the function $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} $ \[ f(x) = \begin{cases} 1, &\text{ if } x \in \mathbb{Q} ;\\ 0, &\text{ if } x \in \mathbb{R} \setminus \mathbb{Q} . \end{cases} \] We will show that the function is not continuous at any real number.
Let $a \in \mathbb{Q} $. We will show that $f$ is not continuous at $a$. Consider a sequence of irrational which converges to $a$. One possible sequence is \[ x_n = a + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{n}, \quad n \in \mathbb{N} . \] Note that for any $n \in \mathbb{N} $, \[ f(x_n) = f \left( a + \frac{\sqrt{2} }{n} \right) = 0. \] So the sequence $f(x_n) \rightarrow 0$ but $f(a) = 1$, thus the function is not continuous at any rational number.
Now let $a \in \mathbb{R} \setminus \mathbb{Q} $ and consider a sequence of rationals converging to $a$ (this can be done as $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R} $). A way to construct the sequence is as follows. Without loss of generality assume that $a > 0$. Between $0$ and $a$, due to density of rationals in reals, there exists a rational number, say $q_1$. Similarly, get another rational number $q_2$ such that $0 < q_1 < q_2 < a$. In the similar way, we get a sequence of increasing rationals $0 < q_1 < q_2 < \dots < q_n < \dots < a$ which converges to $a$. Now to show that $f$ is discontinuous at $a$, note that \[ \lim_{n \to \infty} f(q_n) = \lim_{n \to \infty} 1 = 1, \] whereas, $f(a) = 0$. Thus, $f$ is not continuous at $a$. As $a$ was arbitrarily chosen irrational number, $f$ is not continuous at any irrational number.

Thus $f$ is discontinuous at every real number.

problems.title: f(x) = x^2 is not uniformly continuous on R
problems.problem: Prove that. $f(x) = x^2$ is uniformly continuous on $[0,1]$ but not uniformly continuous on $\mathbb{R} $.
problems.hint:
• A continuous function on a compact set is uniformly continuous.
  • As $x$ is increasing, the graph becomes more straight and hence in the functional value it takes a big jump, which suggests not uniform continuous on $\mathbb{R} $.
problems.solution:
To show that $f(x) = x^2$ is uniformly continuous, let $\varepsilon > 0 $ be given. We need to find $\delta = \delta (\varepsilon ) > 0$ such that for any any $x,y \in [0,1]$ \[ |x - y| < \delta (\varepsilon ) \implies |f(x) - f(y)| < \epsilon . \] Consider \begin{align*} \vert f(x) - f(y) \vert & = \vert x^2 - y^2 \vert \\ & = \vert (x-y)(x+y) \vert \\ & = \vert x-y \vert \cdot \vert x+y \vert \\ & \leq \vert x - y \vert \left( \vert x \vert + \vert y \vert \right) \\ & \leq 2 \vert x - y \vert . \end{align*} Choose $\delta (\varepsilon ) = \frac{\varepsilon }{6}$. If $\vert x - y \vert < \frac{\varepsilon}{6} $, then \begin{align*} \vert f(x) - f(y) \vert \leq 2 \vert x-y \vert < 2 \frac{\varepsilon }{6} = \frac{\varepsilon }{3} < \varepsilon . \end{align*} Thus, the function is uniformly continuous on $[0,1]$. Note that the same proof can be adopt for any bounded set, that is, the function is uniformly continuous on any bounded set.
Now to see that the function is not uniformly continuous on $\mathbb{R} $, to the contrary assume that $x^2$ is uniformly continuous on $\mathbb{R} $. So for any $\varepsilon > 0$, we can find $\delta (\varepsilon ) > 0$ such that \[ \vert x - y \vert < \delta (\varepsilon ) \implies \vert f(x) - f(y) \vert < \varepsilon . \] Choose $\varepsilon = 1$, so there exists $\delta = \delta(\varepsilon ) > 0$ such that \[ \vert x - y \vert < \delta (\varepsilon ) \implies \vert f(x) - f(y) \vert < 1 . \] By Archimedean property, choose $n \in \mathbb{N} $ such that $\delta > \frac{1}{n}$. Take $x = n$ and $y = n + \frac{\delta}{2} $, then \begin{align*} \vert x - y \vert & = \left\vert n - n + \frac{\delta}{2} \right\vert = \left\vert \frac{\delta }{2} \right\vert > \frac{1}{2n} > \varepsilon , \end{align*} a contradiction. Thus, $f(x) = x^2$ is not uniformly continuous on $\mathbb{R} $.

problems.title: NBHM 2005 - Infinite sum of functional value
problems.problem:
Let $f: [0,1] \to  \mathbb{R} $ be a continuous function. Then find 
    \[
        \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n} f \left( \frac{k}{n}. \right) 
    \]
problems.hint:
• Since $f$ is continuous, it is Riemann integrable.
  • Use definite integral as a limit of sum.
problems.solution:
Since $f$ is continuous on the closed interval $[0,1]$, it is Riemann integrable on $[0,1]$. We need to find the sum \[ \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n} f\left(\frac{k}{n}\right). \] This sum differs from the standard Riemann sum \[ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} f\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\frac{1}{n} \] only by the additional term $\frac{1}{n}f(1)$.
Thus we can write \begin{align*} & \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n} f\left(\frac{k}{n}\right) = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} f\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\frac{1}{n} + \frac{1}{n}f(1) \\ \implies & \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n} f\left(\frac{k}{n}\right) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} f\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\frac{1}{n} + \frac{1}{n}f(1) \right] \\ & \kern 2.85cm = \lim_{n \to \infty} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} f\left(\frac{k}{n}\right)\frac{1}{n} + \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n}f(1) \tag{1} \\ & \kern 2.85cm = \int_0^1 f(x) \mathrm{d} x + 0. \end{align*} Note that (1) is valid because $f$ is Riemann integrable and hence both the limit exists. Therefore, \[ \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n} f\left(\frac{k}{n}\right) = \int_0^1 f(x)\,dx. \]

problems.title: An upper bound is greater than or equals to lower bounds
problems.problem: Let $E$ be a nonempty subset of an ordered set $(X, \preccurlyeq)$. Suppose $\alpha $ is a lower bound of $E$ and $\beta $ is an upper bound of $E$. Prove that $\alpha \preccurlyeq \beta $.
problems.hint: • $\alpha$ is a lower bound of $E$ if $\alpha \preccurlyeq x$ for any $x \in E$ and $\beta $ is an upper bound of $E$ if $x \preccurlyeq \beta $ for any $x \in E$.
problems.solution: As $E \neq \varnothing $, take $x_0 \in E$. Since $\alpha $ is a lower bound of $E$, so for $x_0 \in E$ we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:26Jan2026-1} \alpha \preccurlyeq x_0. \end{equation} Since $\beta $ is an upper bound of $E$, so we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:26Jan2026-2} x_0 \preccurlyeq \beta . \end{equation} Combining \eqref{eq:26Jan2026-1} and \eqref{eq:26Jan2026-2}, we get \[ \alpha \preccurlyeq x_0 \preccurlyeq \beta \implies \alpha \preccurlyeq \beta . \]

problems.title: Uniform convergence of the series pi/2 - arctan(n^p ( 1 + x^2))
problems.problem:
Let $p>1$. Show that the series 
    \[
        \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ \frac{\pi }{2} - \arctan \left[ n^p (1 + x^2) \right]  \right] 
    \] 
    converges uniformly on $\mathbb{R} $.
problems.hint:
• For $x > 0$, recall that $\arctan x + \arctan \frac{1}{x} = \frac{\pi}{2} $.
  • For $x>0$, $\arctan x < x$.
  • Use Weierstrass $M$-test.
problems.solution:
Recall that for $x > 0$, \[ \arctan x + \arctan \frac{1}{x} = \frac{\pi}{2}. \] This implies, for any $x \in \mathbb{R} $ \begin{equation}\label{eq:02Feb2026-1} 0 < \frac{\pi}{2} - \arctan \left[ n^p (1 + x^2) \right] = \arctan \frac{1}{n^p(1 + x^2)}. \end{equation}
Consider the function $f(x) = x - \arctan x$ for $x > 0$. Since \[ f'(x) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + x^2} = \frac{x^2}{1 + x^2} > 0, \] so $f$ is a strictly increasing function on $(0, \infty )$. Thus, for $x > 0$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:02Feb2026-2} x - \arctan x > 0 \implies x > \arctan x. \end{equation}
Now the inequality \eqref{eq:02Feb2026-1} simplified to \begin{align*} \frac{\pi}{2} - \arctan [n^p ( 1 + x^2)] < \frac{1}{n^p(1 + x^2)} \leq \frac{1}{n^p} \end{align*} for all $x \in \mathbb{R} $. Since $p> 1$, so the series $\sum_{n} \frac{1}{n^p} $ is convergent. Now the conclusion follows from Weierstrass $M$-test.

problems.title: Uniform Convergence of Functions from Uniform Convergence of Derivatives
problems.problem:
Let \( (f_n) \) be a sequence of differentiable functions on \([a,b]\) such that
  1. \( f_n(a) = 0 \) for all \( n \),
  2. \( (f_n') \) converges uniformly on \([a,b]\).
Show that \( (f_n) \) converges uniformly on \([a,b]\), and that \[ \lim_{n\to\infty} f_n'(x) = \left(\lim_{n\to\infty} f_n(x)\right)'. \]
problems.hint: • Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to express \[ f_n(x)-f_m(x) = \int_a^x \bigl(f_n'(t)-f_m'(t)\bigr)\,dt. \]
problems.solution:
Since \( (f_n') \) converges uniformly on \([a,b]\), it is a Cauchy sequence in the uniform norm. Thus, for every \( \varepsilon > 0 \), there exists \( N \) such that for all \( m,n \ge N \), \[ |f_n'(x) - f_m'(x)| < \frac{\varepsilon}{b-a} \quad \text{for all } x \in [a,b]. \] Fix \( x \in [a,b] \). By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, \[ f_n(x) - f_m(x) = \int_a^x \big( f_n'(t) - f_m'(t) \big)\, dt. \] Taking absolute values, \begin{align*} |f_n(x) - f_m(x)| & \le \int_a^x |f_n'(t) - f_m'(t)|\, dt \\ & \le (b-a)\sup_{t\in[a,b]} |f_n'(t)-f_m'(t)|. \end{align*} Hence, for all \( m,n \ge N \), \[ |f_n(x) - f_m(x)| < \varepsilon. \] Since this bound is independent of \( x \), the sequence \( (f_n) \) is uniformly Cauchy on \([a,b]\), and therefore converges uniformly to some function \( f \).
Now define \[ g(x) = \lim_{n\to\infty} f_n'(x), \] which exists and is continuous since uniform limits of continuous functions are continuous. Using again the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and passing to the limit, \begin{align*} f(x) = \lim_{n\to\infty} f_n(x) & = \lim_{n\to\infty} \int_a^x f_n'(t)\,dt \\ & = \int_a^x g(t)\,dt. \end{align*} Thus \( f \) is differentiable and \[ f'(x) = g(x) = \lim_{n\to\infty} f_n'(x). \]

problems.title: \limsup_{n \to  \infty } (a_n + b_n) \leq \limsup_{n \to  \infty } a_n + \limsup_{n \to  \infty } b_n
problems.problem:
For any two real sequences $\{ a_n \} $ and $\{ b_n \} $, prove that 
    \[
        \limsup_{n \to  \infty } (a_n + b_n) \leq \limsup_{n \to  \infty } a_n + \limsup_{n \to  \infty } b_n.
    \]
problems.hint:
• Recall that \[ \limsup_{n\to\infty} a_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} \sup_{k \ge n} a_k. \] Rewrite the three limsups in the statement using this definition.
  • Fix $n$. For every $k \ge n$, \[ a_k \le \sup_{j \ge n} a_j \quad\text{and}\quad b_k \le \sup_{j \ge n} b_j. \] Add these inequalities and then take the supremum over $k \ge n$.
problems.solution:
Recall that \[ \limsup_{n\to\infty} a_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} \sup_{k \ge n} a_k. \] Define \[ A_n = \sup_{k \ge n} a_k, \qquad B_n = \sup_{k \ge n} b_k. \] Then \[ \limsup_{n \to \infty } a_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} A_n, \qquad \limsup_{n \to \infty } b_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} B_n. \]
For fixed $n$ and every $k \ge n$, \[ a_k \le A_n, \qquad b_k \le B_n. \] Hence \[ a_k + b_k \le A_n + B_n. \] Taking the supremum over $k \ge n$ gives \[ \sup_{k \ge n}(a_k + b_k) \le A_n + B_n. \] Finally, taking limits as $n \to \infty$, \begin{align*} \limsup_{n\to\infty}(a_n + b_n) & = \lim_{n\to\infty} \sup_{k \ge n}(a_k + b_k) \\ & \le \lim_{n\to\infty} (A_n + B_n) \\ & = \limsup_{n\to\infty} a_n + \limsup_{n\to\infty} b_n. \end{align*}

problems.title: Rudin solution - Convergence of Series
problems.problem:
Investigate the behavior (convergence or divergence) of $\sum_n a_n $ if
  1. $a_n = \sqrt{n + 1} - \sqrt{n} $;
  2. $\frac{\sqrt{n + 1} - \sqrt{n} }{n}$;
  3. $a_n = \left( \sqrt[n]{n} - 1 \right)^n $;
  4. $a_n = \frac{1}{1 + z^n}, $ for complex values of $z$.
problems.hint:
• Apply the limit comparison test and use the $p$-series test.
  • For the first one, by rationalizing $a_n \geq \frac{1}{2\sqrt{n+1} }$.
  • For the second one, after rationalizing, $a_n \leq \frac{1}{2n^{\frac{3}{2}}}$.
  • Use induction to show that $\left( \frac{3}{2} \right)^n > n $ for $n \geq 2$ and use the limit comparison test.
  • Split into cases $\vert z \vert \leq 1$ and $\vert z \vert > 1$.
problems.solution:
We study the convergence of $\displaystyle \sum_{n=1}^\infty a_n$ in each case.
  1. $a_n = \sqrt{n+1} - \sqrt{n}$. Note that \begin{align*} \sqrt{n+1} - \sqrt{n} & = (\sqrt{n+1} - \sqrt{n}) \times \frac{(\sqrt{n+1} + \sqrt{n})}{\sqrt{n+1} + \sqrt{n}} \\ & = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n+1} + \sqrt{n} } \\ & \geq \frac{1}{2\sqrt{n+1} }. \end{align*} By the $p$-series test, the series $\sum_{n} \frac{1}{2\sqrt{n+1} }$ diverges, so by the limit comparison test the series $\sum_{n} a_n$ diverges.
  2. $a_n = \dfrac{\sqrt{n+1} - \sqrt{n}}{n}$. Observe that \begin{align*} \left( \sqrt{n+1} - \sqrt{n} \right) \times \frac{\sqrt{n+1} + \sqrt{n}}{\sqrt{n+1} + \sqrt{n}} & = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n+1} + \sqrt{n}} \\ & \leq \frac{1}{2n\sqrt{n} } \\ & = \frac{1}{2 n ^{\frac{3}{2}}} \end{align*} By the $p$-series test, $\sum \frac{1}{2n^{3/2}}$ converges, so by the limit comparison test the series $\sum_{n} a_n$ converges.
  3. $a_n = \left( \sqrt[n]{n} - 1 \right) ^n$. At first we claim that $n < \left( \frac{3}{2} \right)^n $ for $n \geq 2$. We will prove this by induction. Note that for $n = 2$, it is true that \begin{align*} \left( \frac{3}{2} \right) ^2 = 2.25 > 2. \end{align*} Let for $n = k$, we have \[ k < \left( \frac{3}{2} \right) ^k. \] For $n = k + 1$, we have \begin{align*} \left( \frac{3}{2} \right) ^{k+1} = \left( \frac{3}{2} \right) ^k \cdot \frac{3}{2} > \frac{3k}{2}. \end{align*} Since $\frac{3k}{2} - (k + 1) = \frac{3k - 2k - 2}{2} = \frac{k-2}{2} > 0$. Thus, $\frac{3k}{2} > k + 1$ and hence the claim is proved. Now observe that \begin{align*} n < \left( \frac{3}{2} \right) ^n & \implies \sqrt[n]{n} < \frac{3}{2} \\ & \implies \sqrt[n]{n} - 1 < \frac{1}{2} \\ & \implies \left( \sqrt[n]{n} - 1 \right)^n < \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^n. \end{align*} Since the series $\sum_{n} \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) ^n$ is convergent, by the comparison test, the series $\sum_{n} a_n$ will also converge.
  4. $a_n = \frac{1}{1 + z^n}$, where $z \in \mathbb{C} $. We will make two cases. The first one is if $\vert z \vert \leq 1$. Then \begin{align*} \vert 1 + z^n \vert \leq 1 + \vert z \vert ^n \leq 2. \end{align*} Thus, \begin{align*} \vert a_n \vert & = \left\vert \frac{1}{1 + z^n} \right\vert = \frac{1}{\vert 1 + z^n \vert } \\ & \geq \frac{1}{1 + \vert z \vert ^n} \geq \frac{1}{2} \\ & \implies \lim_{n \to \infty} \vert a_n \vert \neq 0 \\ & \implies \lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \neq 0. \end{align*} This implies the series $\sum_{n} a_n$ is divergent. The other case is if $\vert z \vert > 1$. Since $\vert 1 + z^n \vert \geq \left\vert 1- \vert z^n \vert \right\vert $, we have \begin{align*} \vert a_n \vert& = \left\vert \frac{1}{1 + z^n} \right\vert = \frac{1}{\vert 1 + z^n \vert } \\ & \leq \frac{1}{\vert z^n \vert - 1}. \end{align*} Since $\vert z \vert > 1$, assume $\vert z \vert = 1 + r$, for some $r > 0$. So we have \begin{align*} \vert a_n \vert \leq \frac{1}{\vert z \vert ^n - 1} & = \frac{1}{(1 + r)^n - 1} = \frac{1}{1 + nr + \frac{n(n-1)}{2}r^2 + \dots r^n - 1} \\ & = \frac{1}{nr + \frac{n(n+1)}{2}r^2 + \dots + r^n} \leq \frac{2}{n^2r^2} . \end{align*} Since the series $\sum_{n} \frac{1}{n^2}$ is convergent, by the comparison test, the series $\sum_{n} \vert a_n \vert $ is convergent. Since the series is absolutely convergent, it will also converge. Thus, $\sum_{n}a_n $ is convergent.

problems.title: Rudin Solution -  \sum_{n} \frac{\sqrt{a_n} }{n} is convergent
problems.problem:
Prove that the convergence of $\sum_{n} a_n$ implies the convergence of 
    \[
        \sum_{n} \frac{\sqrt{a_n} }{n},
    \] 
    if $a_n \geq 0$.
problems.hint: • Note that $\left( \sqrt{a_n} - \frac{1}{n} \right)^2 \geq 0 $. Expand and use limit comparison test.
problems.solution: Given that $a_n$ is a non-negative sequence and $\sum_{n} a_n$ is convergent. We need to show that the series $\sum_{n} \frac{\sqrt{a_n} }{n}$ is convergent. Note that for any $n \in \mathbb{N} $, \begin{align*} \left( \sqrt{a_n} - \frac{1}{n} \right) ^2 \geq 0 & \implies a_n - 2 \frac{\sqrt{a_n} }{n} + \frac{1}{n^2} \geq 0 \\ & \implies \frac{\sqrt{a_n} }{n} \leq \frac{1}{2} \left( a_n + \frac{1}{n^2} \right). \end{align*} SInce the series $\sum_{n} a_n$ and the series $\sum_{n} \frac{1}{n^2}$ are convergent, so by the limit comparison test, the series $\sum_{n} \frac{\sqrt{a_n} }{n}$ is also convergent.

problems.title: Rudin 3.8 - If $\sum_{n} a_n $ converges, and if $\{ b_n \} $  is monotonic and bounded, prove that $\sum_{n} a_n b_n$ converges
problems.problem: If $\sum_{n} a_n $ converges, and if $\{ b_n \} $  is monotonic and bounded, prove that $\sum_{n} a_n b_n$ converges.
problems.hint:
• Let $A_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k$. Since $\sum a_n$ converges, the sequence $\{A_n\}$ is bounded.
  • Show that $\sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k b_k = A_n b_n + \sum_{k=2}^{n} A_{k-1} (b_{k-1} - b_{k} )$ and use the fact that $\{b_n\}$ is monotone and bounded.
problems.solution:
Let \[ A_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k . \] Since $\sum a_n$ converges, the sequence $\{A_n\}$ of partial sums is bounded. Hence there exists $M > 0$ such that \[ |A_n| \le M \quad \text{for all } n. \] Note that $A_k - A_{k-1} = a_k $. Consider the partial sums \begin{align*} S_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k b_k & = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (A_k - A_{k-1} ) b_k \\ & = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_k b_k - \sum_{k=2}^{n} A_{k-1} b_k \\ & = \sum_{k=2}^{n+1} A_{k-1} b_{k-1} - \sum_{k=2}^{n} A_{k-1} b_k \\ & = A_n b_n + \sum_{k=2}^{n} A_{k-1} (b_{k-1} - b_{k} ). \end{align*}
Since $A_n$ is the partial sum sequence of a convergent sequence, so $\{ A_n \} $ is convergent. Also, $\{ b_n \} $ is monotonic and bounded sequence and hence convergent. So the product sequence $\{ A_n b_n \} $ will also converges. The other series \begin{align*} \left\vert \sum_{k=2}^{n} A_{k-1} (b_{k-1} - b_k) \right\vert & \leq \sum_{k=2}^{n} \vert A_{k-1} \vert \cdot \vert b_{k-1} - b_k \vert \\ & \leq \sum_{k=2}^{n} M \vert b_{k-1} - b_k \vert . \end{align*} Also, since $\{b_n\}$ is monotone and bounded, the series \[ \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} |b_n - b_{n+1}| \] converges (it telescopes). Therefore, the partial sum sequence $\{ S_n \} $ converges, and therefore \[ \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n b_n \] converges.

problems.title: Rudin - Rate of convergence (Problem 3.9)
problems.problem:
Find the radius of convergence of each of the following power series.
  1. $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n^3 z^n$
  2. $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{2^n}{n!} z^n$
  3. $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{2^n}{n^2} z^n$
  4. $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{n^3}{3^n} z^n$.
problems.hint:
• Use the root test and the ratio test to find the radius of convergence.
  • Use the fact that for any $a > 0$, $\lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{a} = 1$ and $\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{a^{n+1}}{a^n} = a$.
problems.solution:
We will find the radius of convergence of each power series using the root test, that is, the radius of convergence is given by \[ R = \frac{1}{\limsup_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|a_n|}}, \] where $a_n$ is the coefficient of $z^n$ in the power series. We will use the following fact: for any $a > 0$, $\lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{a} = 1$. Also, by ratio test, for any $a > 0$, $\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{a^{n+1}}{a^n} = a$.
  1. For the first one, we have \begin{align*} \frac{1}{R} & = \limsup_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|n^3|} \\ & = \limsup_{n \to \infty} n^{3 / n} \\ & = \limsup_{n \to \infty} \left( n^{1 / n} \right)^3 = 1. \end{align*} Thus, the radius of convergence is $R = 1$.
  2. For the second one, we will use the ratio test. Note that \begin{align*} \frac{1}{R} & = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left\vert \frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n} \right\vert \\ & = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{n+1}}{(n+1)!} \cdot \frac{n!}{2^n} \\ & = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2}{n+1} = 0. \end{align*} Thus, the radius of convergence is $R = \infty$.
  3. For the third one, we will use the root test. Even ratio test will also work here. We have \begin{align*} \frac{1}{R} & = \limsup_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{\left|\frac{2^n}{n^2}\right|} \\ & = \limsup_{n\to\infty} \left( \frac{2^n}{n^2} \right)^{1/n} \\ & = \limsup_{n\to\infty} \frac{2}{n^{2/n}} = 2. \end{align*} Thus, the radius of convergence is $R = \frac{1}{2}$.
  4. For the fourth one, we will use the root test. We have \begin{align*} \frac{1}{R} & = \limsup_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{\left|\frac{n^3}{3^n}\right|} \\ & = \limsup_{n\to\infty} \left( \frac{n^3}{3^n} \right)^{1/n} \\ & = \limsup_{n\to\infty} \frac{n^{3/n}}{3} = \frac{1}{3}. \end{align*} Thus, the radius of convergence is $R = 3$.

problems.title: Rudin - Radius of convergence of power series with integer coefficients (Problem 3.10)
problems.problem: Suppose that the coefficients of the power series $\sum_{n} a_n z^n$ are integers, infinitely many of which are distinct from zero. Prove that the radius of convergence of the power series is at most $1$.
problems.hint: • Note that for infinitely many $n$, $\sqrt[n]{|a_n |}\geqslant \sqrt[n]{1}\geqslant 1$.
problems.solution: Let $R$ be the radius of convergence of the power series $\sum_{n} a_n z^n$. Since infinitely many coefficients $a_n$ are distinct from zero, there exists a subsequence $\{ a_{n_k} \} $ such that $a_{n_k} \neq 0$ for all $k$. Note that for any $a > 0$, $\lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{a} = 1$. Thus, we have \begin{align*} \frac{1}{R} & = \limsup_{n\to\infty} \sqrt[n]{|a_n|} \\ & \geq \limsup_{k\to\infty} \sqrt[n_k]{|a_{n_k}|} = 1. \end{align*} Hence, the radius of convergence is at most $1$, that is, $R \leq 1$.

problems.title: Rudin - Problem 3.11 - Divergence and convergence of series
problems.problem:
Suppose $a_n > 0$, $S_n = a_1 + \dots + a_n$, and $\sum_{n} a_n$ diverges.
  1. Prove that $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{1 + a_n} $ diverges.
  2. Prove that \[ \frac{a_{N+1} }{S_{N+1} } + \dots + \frac{a_{N+k} }{S_{N+k} } \geq 1 - \frac{S_N}{S_{N+k} } \] and deduce that $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{S_n} $ diverges.
  3. Prove that \[ \frac{a_n}{S_n^2} \leq \frac{1}{S_{n-1}} - \frac{1}{S_n} \] and deduce that $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{S_n^2} $ converges.
  4. What can be said about \[ \sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{1 + n a_n} \text{ and } \sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{1 + n^2 a_n}? \]
problems.hint:
• Use the limit comparison test for the first part.
  • Use the fact that $S_n$ is increasing and hence for any $j < N + k$, we have $\frac{1}{S_j} \geq \frac{1}{S_{N+k}}$.
problems.solution:
We will study each part separately.
  1. We need to prove that the series $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{1 + a_n}$ diverges. Since the series $\sum_{n} a_n$ diverges, we have the following two cases. If $a_n \not\to 0$, then $\frac{a_n}{1 + a_n} \not\to 0$, and hence the series diverges. The other case is, if $a_n \rightarrow 0$, then we can use the limit comparison test. Note that \begin{align*} \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\frac{a_n}{1 + a_n}}{a_n} & = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{1 + a_n} = 1. \end{align*} Since the series $\sum_{n} a_n$ diverges, by the limit comparison test, the series $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{1 + a_n}$ also diverges.
  2. Since $S_n = a_1 + \dots + a_n$, we have $a_n = S_n - S_{n-1}$, and $a_n > 0 $, so the sequence $\{ S_n \} $ is increasing. Hence, for any $j < N + k$, we have \[ \frac{1}{S_j} \geq \frac{1}{S_{N+k}}. \] Thus, we have \begin{align*} \sum_{j=N+1}^{N+k} \frac{a_j}{S_j} & \geq \sum_{j=N+1}^{N+k} \frac{a_j}{S_{N+k} } = \frac{1}{S_{N+k}} \sum_{j=N+1}^{N+k} a_j \\ & = \frac{1}{S_{N+k}} (S_{N+k} - S_N) = 1 - \frac{S_N}{S_{N+k}}. \end{align*} Thus, we have \[ \frac{a_{N+1} }{S_{N+1} } + \dots + \frac{a_{N+k} }{S_{N+k} } \geq 1 - \frac{S_N}{S_{N+k} } \] As $k \to \infty$, since $\sum_{n} a_n$ diverges, so $S_{N+k} \to \infty$. Hence, we have \[ \lim_{k \to \infty} \left( 1 - \frac{S_N}{S_{N+k}} \right) = 1. \] This shows the tail of the series does not converge to $0$, violating the Cauchy Criterion for convergence.
  3. Note that for any $n$, we have \begin{align*} \frac{a_n}{S_n^2} & = \frac{a_n}{S_{n-1}^2} \cdot \frac{S_{n-1}^2}{S_n^2} \\ & = \frac{a_n}{S_{n-1}^2} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{a_n}{S_{n-1}} \right)^{-2} \\ & \leq \frac{a_n}{S_{n-1}^2} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{a_n}{S_{n-1}} \right)^{-1} \\ & = \frac{a_n}{S_{n-1}^2 + a_n S_{n-1}} \\ & = \frac{a_n}{S_n S_{n-1}} = \frac{1}{S_{n-1}} - \frac{1}{S_n}. \end{align*} Hence, we have \[ \frac{a_n}{S_n^2} \leq \frac{1}{S_{n-1}} - \frac{1}{S_n}. \] Since $\sum_{n} \left( \frac{1}{S_{n-1}} - \frac{1}{S_n} \right)$ converges (it telescopes), by the comparison test, the series $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{S_n^2}$ also converges.
  4. For the first one, we have the both possibilities, that is, it may converge or diverge. For example, if $a_n = 1$ for all $n$, then the series $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{1 + n a_n} = \sum_{n} \frac{1}{1 + n}$ will diverge. On the other hand, if \[ a_n = \begin{cases} 1, &\text{ if } n = 2^k ;\\ \frac{1}{n^2}, &\text{ if } \text{ otherwise}. \end{cases}, \] then $\sum_{n} a_n$ diverges but \begin{align*} \frac{a_n}{1 + n a_n} = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{1 + 2^k}, &\text{ if } n = 2^k ;\\ \frac{1/n^2}{1 + n/n^2} = \frac{1}{n^2 + n}, &\text{ if } \text{ otherwise}. \end{cases} \end{align*} Since the series $\sum_{n} \frac{1}{1 + 2^k}$ and $\sum_{n} \frac{1}{n^2 + n}$ both converge, by the comparison test, the series $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{1 + n a_n}$ also converges. For the second one, we have \[ \frac{a_n}{1 + n^2 a_n} < \frac{a_n}{n^2 a_n} = \frac{1}{n^2}. \] Since the series $\sum_{n} \frac{1}{n^2}$ converges, by the comparison test, the series $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{1 + n^2 a_n}$ also converges.

problems.title: Rudin - Problem 3.12 -  Divergence and convergence of series
problems.problem:
Suppose $a_n > 0$ and $\sum_{n} a_n$ converges. Put \[ r_n = \sum_{m=n}^{\infty} a_m. \]
  1. Prove that \[ \frac{a_m}{r_m} + \dots + \frac{a_n}{r_n} > 1 - \frac{r_{n+1}}{r_m} \] if $m < n$, and deduce that $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{r_n}$ diverges.
  2. Prove that \[ \frac{a_n}{\sqrt{r_n} } < 2 \left( \sqrt{r_{n}} - \sqrt{r_{n+1} } \right) \] and deduce that $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{\sqrt{r_n} }$ converges.
problems.hint:
• For (a), note that $a_n = r_n - r_{n+1}$. Since $r_k$ is strictly decreasing, compare $1/r_k$ to $1/r_m$ for the indices in the sum.
  • For (b), use the difference of squares identity to rewrite $a_n = (\sqrt{r_n} - \sqrt{r_{n+1}})(\sqrt{r_n} + \sqrt{r_{n+1}})$ and bound the second factor.
problems.solution:
1. Since $r_n = \sum_{k=n}^{\infty} a_k$, we have $a_n = r_n - r_{n+1}$. Because $a_n > 0$, the sequence $\{r_n\}$ is strictly decreasing and $\lim_{n \to \infty} r_n = 0$. To see this note that $r_n$ is the tail of a convergent series, so it must go to zero. For $m \leq k \leq n$, we have $r_k \leq r_m$, which implies $\frac{1}{r_k} \geq \frac{1}{r_m}$. Thus: \begin{align*} \sum_{k=m}^{n} \frac{a_k}{r_k} & \geq \sum_{k=m}^{n} \frac{a_k}{r_m} = \sum_{k=m}^{n} \frac{r_k - r_{k+1}}{r_m} \\ & = \frac{r_m - r_{n+1}}{r_m} = 1 - \frac{r_{n+1}}{r_m}. \end{align*} Now we will show that $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{r_n}$ diverges. If $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{r_n}$ converges, then by the Cauchy Criterion, for $\varepsilon = \frac{1}{2}$, there exists $N$ such that for all $m, n > N$, we have \[ \left| \sum_{k=m}^{n} \frac{a_k}{r_k} \right| < \frac{1}{2}. \] However, from the inequality we just proved, we have \[ \sum_{k=m}^{n} \frac{a_k}{r_k} > 1 - \frac{r_{n+1}}{r_m}. \] As $n \to \infty$, since $r_{n+1} \to 0$, we have \[ \lim_{n \to \infty} \left( 1 - \frac{r_{n+1}}{r_m} \right) = 1. \] This contradicts the Cauchy Criterion for convergence, so $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{r_n}$ must diverge.
  2. Note that we can write \[ a_n = r_n - r_{n+1} = (\sqrt{r_n} - \sqrt{r_{n+1}})(\sqrt{r_n} + \sqrt{r_{n+1}}). \] Since $\sqrt{r_{n+1}} < \sqrt{r_n}$, it follows that $\sqrt{r_n} + \sqrt{r_{n+1}} < 2\sqrt{r_n}$. Thus we get, \[ \frac{a_n}{\sqrt{r_n}} = \frac{(\sqrt{r_n} - \sqrt{r_{n+1}})(\sqrt{r_n} + \sqrt{r_{n+1}})}{\sqrt{r_n}} < 2(\sqrt{r_n} - \sqrt{r_{n+1}}) \] Therefore, the partial sums $S_N$ of the series $\sum_{n} \frac{a_n}{\sqrt{r_n}}$ satisfy \[ \sum_{n=1}^{N} \frac{a_n}{\sqrt{r_n}} < \sum_{n=1}^{N} 2(\sqrt{r_n} - \sqrt{r_{n+1}}) = 2(\sqrt{r_1} - \sqrt{r_{N+1}}) < 2\sqrt{r_1} \] and hence, as a series with positive terms and bounded partial sums, it converges.

problems.title: Rudin Real Analysis - Prove that the Cauchy product of two absolutely convergent series converges absolutely
problems.problem: Prove that the Cauchy product of two absolutely convergent series converges absolutely.
problems.hint: • Let $A_N = \sum_{n=0}^N |a_n|$ and $B_N = \sum_{n=0}^N |b_n|$. Consider the partial sum of the absolute Cauchy product, $C^*_N = \sum_{n=0}^N |c_n|$, and try to bound it by the product $A_N B_N$.
problems.solution:
Let $\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n$ and $\sum_{n=0}^\infty b_n$ be absolutely convergent series. Let $A = \sum_{n=0}^\infty |a_n|$ and $B = \sum_{n=0}^\infty |b_n|$. The Cauchy product is defined by $c_n = \sum_{k=0}^n a_k b_{n-k}$. We want to show $\sum_{n=0}^\infty |c_n| < \infty$.
Consider the partial sum of the absolute values: \[ \sum_{n=0}^N |c_n| = \sum_{n=0}^N \left| \sum_{k=0}^n a_k b_{n-k} \right| \leq \sum_{n=0}^N \sum_{k=0}^n |a_k| |b_{n-k}|. \] The double sum on the right represents the sum of all terms $|a_j||b_k|$ such that $j + k \leq N$. This region is a triangle in the indices $(j, k)$. We can bound this by summing over the entire square region where $0 \leq j \leq N$ and $0 \leq k \leq N$: \[ \sum_{n=0}^N \sum_{k=0}^n |a_k| |b_{n-k}| \leq \left( \sum_{j=0}^N |a_j| \right) \left( \sum_{k=0}^N |b_k| \right). \]
Since $\sum |a_n|$ and $\sum |b_n|$ converge to $A$ and $B$ respectively, we have: \[ \sum_{n=0}^N |c_n| \leq A_N B_N \leq A \cdot B. \] The partial sums of $\sum |c_n|$ are monotonically increasing (since its sum of non-negative terms) and bounded above by $AB$. By the Monotone Convergence Theorem, the series $\sum |c_n|$ converges. Thus, the Cauchy product converges absolutely.

problems.title: Uniform convergence of series of continuous functions
problems.problem: Let $\{ f_n \} $ be a sequence of continuous functions such that $\sum_{n} f_n$ converges uniformly to a function $f$ on a set $A$. Prove that $f$ is continuous on $A$.
problems.hint: • Show that the sequence of partial sums $F_N(x) = \sum_{n=1}^N f_n(x)$ converges uniformly to $f$ on $A$. Since each $F_N$ is continuous, use the fact that a uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous.
problems.solution:
Consider the partial sums \[ F_N(x) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} f_n(x). \] Given that the sequence $\{ F_n \} $ converges uniformly to $f$ on $A$. It is enough to prove that the sequence of continuous functions $\{ F_{n} \} $ which converges to $F$ uniformly, then $F$ is continuous.
Let $\varepsilon > 0$ be given. Since $F_n \to F$ uniformly, there exists $N$ such that for all $n \geq N$, we have \[ |F_n(x) - F(x)| < \frac{\varepsilon}{3} \quad \text{for all } x \in A. \] Since $F_N$ is continuous, there exists $\delta > 0$ such that \[ |F_N(x) - F_N(y)| < \frac{\varepsilon}{3} \quad \text{whenever } |x - y| < \delta. \] Now, for any $x, y \in A$ with $|x - y| < \delta$, we have \begin{align*} |F(x) - F(y)| & \leq |F(x) - F_N(x)| + |F_N(x) - F_N(y)| + |F_N(y) - F(y)| \\ & < \frac{\varepsilon}{3} + \frac{\varepsilon}{3} + \frac{\varepsilon}{3} = \varepsilon. \end{align*} This shows that $F$ is continuous on $A$.

problems.title: Integrability of a function f(x,y) = xy/(x^2 + y^2)^2 defined on the unit square
problems.problem:
Let $f(x,y)$ be a function defined on $[0,1]^2$ as follows: 
    \[
        f(x,y) = 
        \begin{cases}
            \frac{xy}{(x^2 + y^2)^{2}}, &\text{ if } (x,y) \neq (0,0);\\
            0, &\text{ if } (x,y) = (0,0).
        \end{cases}
    \] 
    Determine if $f$ is integrable.
problems.hint: • Use polar coordinates to evaluate the integral of $f$ over the unit disc in the first quadrant.
problems.solution:
Let $D$ be the unit disc in $\mathbb{R} ^2$ and $I = [0,1]$ . We will switch to the polar coordinates to evaluate the integral of $f$ over $D$. Note that in polar coordinates, we have $x = r \cos \theta$ and $y = r \sin \theta$. We will be integrating over the region $0 \leq r \leq 1$ and $0 \leq \theta \leq \pi/2$, that is, in the first quadrant $Q_1$.
Since $D \cap Q_1 \subseteq I^2$, we have \begin{align*} \int_I^2 f(x,y) \mathrm{d} A & \geq \geq \int_{D \cap Q_1} f(x,y) \mathrm{d} A \\ & = \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{r^2 \cos \theta \sin \theta}{r^4} r \mathrm{d} r \mathrm{d} \theta \\ & = \left(\int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos \theta \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta\right) \left(\int_{0}^{1} \frac{1}{r} \mathrm{d} r\right) \\ & = \left( \int_0^1 u \mathrm{d} u \right) \left( \int_0^1 \frac{1}{r}\mathrm{d} r \right) \quad u = \sin \theta \\ & = \frac{1}{2} \left( \int_0^1 \frac{1}{r}\mathrm{d} r \right) \rightarrow \infty. \end{align*} Thus, the integral of $f$ over $I^2$ diverges, and hence $f$ is not integrable over $I^2$.

problems.title: For a differentiable function, find \lim_{x \to a} \frac{a^n f(x) - x^n f(a)}{x - a}
problems.problem:
Assume that $f$ is differentiable at $a$. Evaluate 
    \[
        \lim_{x \to a} \frac{a^n f(x) - x^n f(a)}{x - a}, \quad n \in \mathbb{N} .
    \]
problems.hint: • Rewrite the expression \[ \frac{a^n f(x) - x^n f(a)}{x - a} = \frac{a^n f(x) - a^n f(a) + a^n f(a) - x^n f(a)}{x - a} \]
problems.solution:
Since $f$ is differentiable at $a$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:04May2026-1} \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a } = f'(a). \end{equation} Rearranging the expression we want to evaluate, we get \begin{align*} \frac{a^n f(x) - x^n f(a)}{x - a} & = \frac{a^n f(x) - a^n f(a) + a^n f(a) - x^n f(a)}{x - a} \\ & = a^n \cdot \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} - f(a) \cdot \frac{x^n - a^n}{x - a}. \end{align*}
Now, we will evaluate the limit of each term separately. For the first term, using equation \eqref{eq:04May2026-1}, we have \[ \lim_{x \to a} a^n \cdot \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} = a^n f'(a). \] For the second term, we have \begin{align*} \lim_{x \to a} f(a) \cdot \frac{x^n - a^n}{x - a} & = f(a) \lim_{x \to a} \frac{x^n - a^n}{x - a} \\ & = f(a) \lim_{x \to a} \frac{(x - a)(x^{n-1} + x^{n-2}a + \dots + a^{n-1})}{x - a} \\ & = f(a) \lim_{x \to a} (x^{n-1} + x^{n-2}a + \dots + a^{n-1}) \\ & = f(a) \cdot n a^{n-1}. \end{align*} Combining these results, we get \[ \lim_{x \to a} \frac{a^n f(x) - x^n f(a)}{x - a} = a^n f'(a) + nf(a) a^{n-1}. \]

problems.title: Differentiability implies continuity
problems.problem: Prove that every differentiable function is continuous.
problems.hint: • Note that \[ \lim_{x \to a} f(x) - f(a) = \lim_{x \to a} \left( (f(x) - f(a)) \cdot \left( \frac{x-a}{x-a} \right) \right). \]
problems.solution:
Let $f: A \subseteq \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} $ be a differentiable function at a point $a \in A$. We will show that $f$ is continuous at $a$. Since $f$ is differentiable at $a$, we have \begin{equation}\label{eq:11May2026-1} f'(a) = \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a}. \end{equation}
We need to show that $\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$. Note that \begin{align*} \lim_{x \to a} f(x) - f(a) & = \lim_{x \to a} \left( (f(x) - f(a)) \cdot \left( \frac{x-a}{x-a} \right) \right) \\ & = \lim_{x \to a} \left( \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} \cdot (x - a) \right) \\ & = \left( \lim_{x \to a} \frac{f(x) - f(a)}{x - a} \right) \cdot \left( \lim_{x \to a} (x - a) \right) \\ & = f'(a) \cdot 0 = 0. \end{align*} Thus, we have $\lim_{x \to a} f(x) - f(a) = 0$, which implies $\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$. Hence, $f$ is continuous at $a$. Since $a$ was an arbitrary point in $A$, we conclude that $f$ is continuous on $A$.

problems.title: Check if the following functions are uniformly continuous on the given intervals
problems.problem:
Check if the following functions are uniformly continuous on the given intervals.
  1. $f(x) = \ln x$, $x \in (0,1)$.
  2. $f(x) = x \sin x$, $x \in [0, \infty)$.
  3. $f(x) = \frac{1}{x^2 + 1}$, $x \in \mathbb{R} $.
problems.hint:
• Choose $x = e^{-n} $ and $y = e^{-(n + 1)}$ for $n \in \mathbb{N}$ to show that $f(x) = \ln x$ is not uniformly continuous on $(0,1)$.
  • Choose $x = 2n\pi$ and $y = 2n\pi + \frac{1}{n}$ for $n \in \mathbb{N}$ to show that $f(x) = x \sin x$ is not uniformly continuous on $[0, \infty)$.
  • Show that $f$ is Lipschitz on $[1, \infty )$ and hence uniformly continuous on $[1, \infty)$.
problems.solution:
Recall that a function is uniformly continuous on an interval if for every $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that for all $x, y$ in the interval, if $|x - y| < \delta$, then $|f(x) - f(y)| < \varepsilon$. We will check each function separately.
  1. The given function is $f(x) = \ln x$, $x \in (0,1)$. We will show that this function is NOT uniformly continuous on $(0,1)$. For that let $\varepsilon = 1$ and $x = e^{-n} $ and $y = e^{-(n+1)} $ for $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Then, we have \[ |x - y| = \left\vert e^{-n} - e^{-(n+1)} \right\vert = e^{-n} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{e} \right) \to 0 \quad \text{as } n \to \infty. \] This implies for any $\delta > 0$, we can find $x, y \in (0,1)$ such that $|x - y| < \delta$. However, we have \[ |f(x) - f(y)| = |\ln(e^{-n}) - \ln(e^{-(n+1)})| = 1 \nless \varepsilon. \] Thus, the function $f(x) = \ln x$ is not uniformly continuous on $(0,1)$.
  2. The given function is $f(x) = x \sin x$, $x \in [0, \infty)$. We will show that this function is NOT uniformly continuous on $[0, \infty)$. For that let $\varepsilon = 1$ and $x = 2n\pi$ and $y = 2n \pi + \frac{1}{n}$ for $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Then, we have \[ |x - y| = \frac{1}{n} \to 0 \quad \text{as } n \to \infty. \] This implies for any $\delta > 0$, we can find $x, y \in [0, \infty)$ such that $|x - y| < \delta$. However, we have \begin{align*} |f(x) - f(y)| & = \left\vert f(2n\pi) - f\left( 2n\pi + \frac{1}{n} \right) \right\vert \\ & = \left\vert 0 - \left( 2n\pi + \frac{1}{n} \right) \sin\left( 2n\pi + \frac{1}{n} \right) \right\vert \\ & = \left( 2n\pi + \frac{1}{n} \right) \left\vert \sin\left( 2n\pi + \frac{1}{n} \right) \right\vert \\ & = \left( 2n\pi + \frac{1}{n} \right) \left\vert \sin\left( \frac{1}{n} \right) \right\vert \\ & \approx \left( 2n\pi + \frac{1}{n} \right) \cdot \frac{1}{n} = 2\pi + \frac{1}{n^2} \to 2\pi \quad \text{as } n \to \infty. \end{align*} Thus, the function $f(x) = x \sin x$ is not uniformly continuous on $[0, \infty)$.
  3. The given function is $f(x) = \frac{1}{x^2 + 1}$, $x \in \mathbb{R} $. We will show that this function is uniformly continuous on $\mathbb{R}$. Observe that the given function is an even function and $f$ is uniformly continuous on $[-1,1]$ (as continuous functions on closed intervals are uniformly continuous). So it is enough to show that $f$ is uniformly continuous on $[1, \infty)$.
    Note that the derivative of the function is \[ f'(x) = -\frac{2x}{(x^2 + 1)^2}. \] As \[ x \geq 1 \implies 2x \leq x^2 + 1 \leq (x^2 + 1)^2 \implies |f'(x)| = \left\vert \frac{2x}{(x^2 + 1)^2} \right\vert \leq 1. \] Since the derivative is bounded on $[1, \infty )$, it follows that the function is Lipschitz continuous and hence the function is uniformly continuous on $[1, \infty)$. Therefore, the function $f(x) = \frac{1}{x^2 + 1}$ is uniformly continuous on $\mathbb{R}$.

problems.title: Convergence of a sequence defined by a recursive relation
problems.problem:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence of real numbers such that $x_1 > 0$ and 
    \[
        x_{n+ 1} = \frac{1 + x_n}{2 + x_n}, \quad  n \in \mathbb{N}.
    \] 
    Prove that the sequence $(x_n)$ converges and find its limit.
problems.hint: • Show that the sequence is bounded and monotone. Then use the Monotone Convergence Theorem to conclude that the sequence converges. Finally, find the limit by solving the equation $L = \frac{1 + L}{2 + L}$.
problems.solution:
Let $(x_n)$ be a sequence defined by $x_1 > 0$ and $x_{n+1} = \frac{1 + x_n}{2 + x_n}$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$. We will show that the sequence $(x_n)$ converges and find its limit.
First, we will show that the sequence is bounded. Note that for any $n$, we have \[ x_{n+1} = \frac{1 + x_n}{2 + x_n} < 1, \] since $x_n > 0$. Thus, the sequence is bounded above by $1$. Next, we will show that the sequence is bounded below by $0$. Since $x_1 > 0$, we have \[ x_2 = \frac{1 + x_1}{2 + x_1} > 0. \] By induction, assume that $x_n > 0$ for some $n$. Then, \[ x_{n+1} = \frac{1 + x_n}{2 + x_n} > 0. \] Thus, by induction, we have $x_n > 0$ for all $n$. Therefore, the sequence $(x_n)$ is bounded below by $0$ and above by $1$.
Next, we will show that the sequence is monotone. Note that \[ x_{n+1} - x_n = \frac{1 + x_n}{2 + x_n} - x_n = \frac{1 + x_n - x_n(2 + x_n)}{2 + x_n} = \frac{1 - x_n^2}{2 + x_n}. \] Since $x_n > 0$, we have $x_n^2 > 0$, which implies that $1 - x_n^2 < 1$. Thus, we have \[ x_{n+1} - x_n < 0, \] which means that the sequence $(x_n)$ is decreasing.
Since the sequence $(x_n)$ is bounded and monotone, it converges. Let $L$ be the limit of the sequence. Then, taking the limit on both sides of the recursive relation, we get \[ L = \frac{1 + L}{2 + L}. \] Rearranging this equation, we get \[ L(2 + L) = 1 + L \implies 2L + L^2 = 1 + L \implies L^2 + L - 1 = 0. \] Solving this quadratic equation, we get \[ L = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 4}}{2} = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2}. \] Since $L$ must be positive (as the sequence is bounded below by $0$), we take the positive root, which gives us \[ L = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{5}}{2}. \] Therefore, the sequence $(x_n)$ converges to $\frac{-1 + \sqrt{5}}{2}$.

problems.title: Bartle and Sherbert 2.3.1: Lower and Upper Bounds of the set $S_1 = { x \in \mathbb{R} : x \geq 0 }$
problems.problem: Let $S_1 = \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : x \geq  0 \} $. Show in detail that the set $S_1$ has lower bounds, but no upper bounds. Show that $\inf S_1 = 0$.
problems.hint:
• To show that $S_1$ has lower bounds, note that any negative number is a lower bound of $S_1$. In particular, $0$ is a lower bound of $S_1$.
  • To show that $S_1$ has no upper bounds, take any real number $M$ and show that there exists an element in $S_1$ that is greater than $M$.
  • To show that $\inf S_1 = 0$, first show that $0$ is a lower bound of $S_1$. Then, let $\beta$ be any lower bound of $S_1$ and show that $\beta \leq 0$.
problems.solution:
The set $S_1 = \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : x \geq 0 \}$ consists of all non-negative real numbers.
First, we will show that $S_1$ has lower bounds. Recall that $\alpha \in \mathbb{R} $ is a lower bound of $S_1$ if given any $x \in S_1$ we have $\alpha \leq x$. We claim that $0$ is a lower bound. To see this, let $x \in S_1$ be arbitrary. By the definition of $S_1$, we have $x \geq 0$. Thus, we have $0 \leq x$, which means that $0$ is a lower bound of $S_1$. In fact, any negative number is also a lower bound of $S_1$, since for any negative number $\alpha < 0$ and any $x \in S_1$, we have $\alpha < 0 \leq x$. Therefore, $S_1$ has infinitely many lower bounds.
Next, we will show that $S_1$ has no upper bounds. Recall that $\alpha\in \mathbb{R} $ is an upper bound of $S_1$ if given any $x \in S_1$ we have $\alpha \geq x$. To show $S_1$ does not have an upper bound, take a real number, say $M$. Since $M$ is an upper bound of $S_1$, $M \geq 0$ (as $0 \in S_1$). Now, consider the number $M + 1$. We have $M + 1 > M \geq 0$, which means that $M + 1 \in S_1$. However, $M + 1 > M$, which contradicts the assumption that $M$ is an upper bound of $S_1$. Thus, there are no upper bounds of $S_1$.
Finally, we will show that $\inf S_1 = 0$. Recall that $\alpha \in \mathbb{R} $ is the infimum (or the greatest lower bound) of $S_1$ if
  1. $\alpha $ is a lower bound of $S_1$, and
  2. if $\beta$ is any lower bound of $S_1$, then $\beta \leq \alpha$.
We have already shown that $0$ is a lower bound of $S_1$. Now, let $\beta$ be any lower bound of $S_1$. Since $\beta$ is a lower bound, we have $\beta \leq x$ for all $x \in S_1$. In particular, since $0 \in S_1$, we have $\beta \leq 0$. Thus, we have shown that if $\beta$ is any lower bound of $S_1$, then $\beta \leq 0$. Therefore, $0$ is the greatest lower bound (or infimum) of $S_1$, and hence $\inf S_1 = 0$.

problems.title: Bartle and Sherbert 2.3.2: Lower and Upper Bounds of the set $S_2 = { x \in \mathbb{R} : x > 0 }$
problems.problem: Let $S_2 = \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : x > 0 \} $. Does $S_2$ have lower bounds? Does $S_2$ have upper bounds? Does $\inf S_2$ exist? Does $\sup S_2$ exist? Prove your statements.
problems.hint: • Go by the definitions of lower bounds, upper bounds, infimum, and supremum to answer the questions. (Look at the previous problem on real analysis (June 1, 2026) for reference.)
problems.solution:
The set $S_2 = \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : x > 0 \}$ consists of all positive real numbers.
First, we will show that $S_2$ has lower bounds. Recall that $\alpha \in \mathbb{R} $ is a lower bound of $S_2$ if given any $x \in S_2$ we have $\alpha \leq x$. We claim that $0$ is a lower bound. To see this, let $x \in S_2$ be arbitrary. By the definition of $S_2$, we have $x > 0$, which means that $0$ is a lower bound of $S_2$. In fact, any negative number is also a lower bound of $S_2$, since for any negative number $\alpha < 0$ and any $x \in S_2$, we have $\alpha < 0 < x$. Therefore, $S_2$ has infinitely many lower bounds.
Next, we will show that $S_2$ has no upper bounds. Recall that $\alpha\in \mathbb{R} $ is an upper bound of $S_2$ if given any $x \in S_2$ we have $\alpha \geq x$. To show $S_2$ does not have an upper bound, take a real number, say $M$. Since $M$ is an upper bound of $S_2$, we must have $M > 0$ (as all elements of $S_2$ are positive). Now, consider the number $M + 1$. We have $M + 1 > M > 0$, which means that $M + 1 \in S_2$. However, $M + 1 > M$, which contradicts the assumption that $M$ is an upper bound of $S_2$. Thus, there are no upper bounds of $S_2$.
Finally, we will show that $\inf S_2 = 0$. Recall that $\alpha \in \mathbb{R} $ is the infimum (or the greatest lower bound) of $S_2$ if
  1. $\alpha $ is a lower bound of $S_2$, and
  2. if $\beta$ is any lower bound of $S_2$, then $\beta \leq \alpha$.
We have already shown that $0$ is a lower bound of $S_2$. Now, let $\beta$ be any lower bound of $S_2$. We need to show that $\beta \leq 0$. Suppose not, that is, $\beta > 0$. Then $\beta\in S_2$. Note that \[ 0 < \frac{\beta}{2} < \beta \implies \frac{\beta}{2} \in S_2 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\beta}{2} > \beta, \] a contradiction to the assumption that $\beta$ is a lower bound of $S_2$. Therefore, $\beta \leq 0$. Thus, we have shown that if $\beta$ is any lower bound of $S_2$, then $\beta \leq 0$. Therefore, $0$ is the greatest lower bound (or infimum) of $S_2$, and hence $\inf S_2 = 0.$
Since $S_2$ has no upper bounds, it follows that $\sup S_2$ does not exist.

problems.title: Bartle and Sherbert 2.3.3: Lower and Upper Bounds of the set $S_3 = { \frac{1}{n}: n \in \mathbb{N} }$
problems.problem: Let $S_3 = \left\{ \frac{1}{n}: n \in \mathbb{N}  \right\} .$ Show that $\sup S_3 = 1$ and $\inf S_3 \geq 0$. (It will follow from the Archimedean property in Section 2.4 that $\inf S_3 = 0$.)
problems.hint: • Look at the set $S_3$ and note that $1 \in S_3$. This will help you to show that $\sup S_3 = 1$. To show that $\inf S_3 \geq 0$, note that $0$ is a lower bound of $S_3$.
problems.solution:
Given that the set \[ S_3 = \left\{ \frac{1}{n}: n \in \mathbb{N} \right\} = \left\{ 1, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{3}, \dots \right\}. \]
First we will show that $\sup S_3 = 1$. We claim that $1$ is the supremum of $S_3$. To see this, first note that $1$ is an upper bound of $S_3$, since for any $n \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $n \geq 1 \implies \frac{1}{n} \leq 1$. Now, let $\beta$ be any upper bound of $S_3$. We need to show that $1 \leq \beta$. Since $\beta$ is an upper bound, we have $\beta \geq \frac{1}{n}$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$. In particular, since $\frac{1}{1} = 1 \in S_3$, we have $\beta \geq 1$. Thus, we have shown that if $\beta$ is any upper bound of $S_3$, then $1 \leq \beta$. Therefore, $1$ is the least upper bound (or supremum) of $S_3$, and hence $\sup S_3 = 1$.
Next, we will show that $\inf S_3 \geq 0$. Let $\alpha = \inf S_3$. So we will show that $\alpha \geq 0$. First, we claim that $0$ is a lower bound for $S_3$. To see this, let $x \in S_3$ be arbitrary. By the definition of $S_3$, we have $x = \frac{1}{n}$ for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$. Since $n \geq 1$, we have $\frac{1}{n} > 0$. Since $\alpha = \inf S_3$, then by definition of the infimum, $\alpha \geq 0$.

problems.title: Bartle and Sherbert 2.3.4: Lower and Upper Bounds of the set $S_1 = { x \in \mathbb{R} : x \geq 0 }$
problems.problem: Let $S_4 = \left\{ 1 - \frac{(-1)^n}{n}: n \in \mathbb{N}  \right\} .$ Find $\inf S_4$ and $\sup S_4$.
problems.hint: • Look at the odd and even terms of the sequence $x_n = 1 - \frac{(-1)^n}{n}$ separately. Note that the odd terms are greater than $1$ and the even terms are less than $1$. Use this to find the infimum and supremum of $S_4$.
problems.solution:
The set $S_4 = \left\{ x_n = 1 - \frac{(-1)^n}{n}: n \in \mathbb{N} \right\}.$ Note that \begin{align*} x_{2n} & = 1 - \frac{(-1)^{2n}}{2n} = 1 - \frac{1}{2n} < 1, \\ x_{2n-1} & = 1 - \frac{(-1)^{2n-1}}{2n-1} = 1 + \frac{1}{2n-1} > 1. \end{align*}
We claim that $\sup S_4 = 2$. Since $x_{2n} < 1 < 2$. Now note that $x_1 = 2$ and for $m > n$ we have $\frac{1}{2m + 1} < \frac{1}{2n + 1}$. Using this, we have \[ x_{2m + 1} = 1 + \frac{1}{2m + 1} < 1 + \frac{1}{2n + 1} = x_{2n + 1}. \] Therefore, for any $n \geq 1$, we have $x_{2n + 1} < x_1 = 2$. Thus, $2$ is an upper bound of $S_4$.

Now, let $\beta$ be any upper bound of $S_4$. We need to show that $\beta \geq 2$. Since $\beta$ is an upper bound, we have $\beta \geq x_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$. In particular, since $x_1 = 2 \in S_4$, we have $\beta \geq 2$. Therefore, $\sup S_4 = 2$.
Next, we claim that $\inf S_4 = \frac{1}{2}$. Note that all the odd terms or the sequence $x_{2n-1}$ are greater than $1$ and hence greater than $\frac{1}{2}$. For the even terms, note that $x_2 = \frac{1}{2}$ and for $m > n$ we have $\frac{1}{2m} < \frac{1}{2n}$. Using this, we have \[ x_{2m} = 1 - \frac{1}{2m} > 1 - \frac{1}{2n} = x_{2n}. \] Therefore, for any $n \geq 1$, we have $x_{2n} > x_2 = \frac{1}{2}$. Thus, $\frac{1}{2}$ is a lower bound of $S_4$.

Now, let $\alpha$ be any lower bound of $S_4$. We need to show that $\alpha \leq \frac{1}{2}$. Since $\alpha$ is a lower bound, we have $\alpha \leq x_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$. In particular, since $x_2 = \frac{1}{2} \in S_4$, we have $\alpha \leq \frac{1}{2}$. Therefore, $\inf S_4 = \frac{1}{2}$.

problems.title: Bartle and Sherbert 2.3.5: Lower and Upper Bounds of the set $S_5 = { x \in \mathbb{R} : x^2 - 2x - 5 < 0 }$
problems.problem:
Find the infimum and supremum, if they exist, of each of the following sets.
  1. $A\coloneqq \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : 2x + 5 > 0 \} $,
  2. $B \coloneqq \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : x + 2 \geq x^2 \} $,
  3. $C \coloneqq \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : x < \frac{1}{x} \} $,
  4. $D \coloneqq \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : x^2 - 2x - 5 < 0 \} $.
problems.hint: • Solve the inequalities to find the sets $A, B, C$ and $D$. Then use the definitions of infimum and supremum to find them for each set.
problems.solution:
1. The set $A = \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : 2x + 5 > 0 \}$. We can write \begin{align*} x \in A & \implies 2x + 5 > 0 \\ & \implies 2x > -5 \\ & \implies x > -\frac{5}{2}. \end{align*} Thus, $A = \left( -\frac{5}{2}, \infty \right)$. The infimum of $A$ is $\inf A = -\frac{5}{2}$ and the supremum does not exist since the set is unbounded above.
  2. The set $B = \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : x + 2 \geq x^2 \}$. We have \begin{align*} x \in B & \implies x + 2 \geq x^2 \\ & \implies 0 \geq x^2 - x - 2 \\ & \implies 0 \geq (x - 2)(x + 1) \\ & \implies (x - 2)(x + 1) \leq 0. \end{align*} Thus, $B = [-1, 2]$. The infimum of $B$ is $\inf B = -1$ and the supremum is $\sup B = 2$.
  3. The set $C = \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : x < \frac{1}{x} \}$. So, we have \begin{align*} x \in C & \implies x < \frac{1}{x} \\ & \implies x - \frac{1}{x} < 0 \\ & \implies \frac{x^2 - 1}{x} < 0 \\ & \implies \frac{(x - 1)(x + 1)}{x} < 0. \end{align*} Thus, the solution to this inequality is $x \in (-1, 0) \cup (0, 1)$. Therefore, $C = (-1, 0) \cup (0, 1)$. The infimum of $C$ is $\inf C = -1$ and the supremum is $\sup C = 1$.
  4. The set $D = \{ x \in \mathbb{R} : x^2 - 2x - 5 < 0 \}$. So, \begin{align*} x \in D & \implies x^2 - 2x - 5 < 0 \\ & \implies (x - (1 + \sqrt{6}))(x - (1 - \sqrt{6})) < 0. \end{align*} Thus, the solution to this inequality is $x \in (1 - \sqrt{6}, 1 + \sqrt{6})$. Therefore, $D = (1 - \sqrt{6}, 1 + \sqrt{6})$. The infimum of $D$ is $\inf D = 1 - \sqrt{6}$ and the supremum is $\sup D = 1 + \sqrt{6}$.

problems.title: Bartle and Sherbert 2.3.6: inf S = - sup (-S)
problems.problem:
Let $S$ be a nonempty subset of $\mathbb{R} $ that is bounded below. Prove that 
    \[
        \inf S = -\sup \{ -s: s \in S \} .
    \]
problems.hint: • Let $T = \{ -s: s \in S \}$. Show that $T$ is bounded above and hence has a supremum. Then use the definition of infimum and supremum to show that $\inf S = -\sup T$.
problems.solution:
First of all we will show that the set \[ T \coloneqq \{ -s: s \in S \} \] is bounded above. Since $S$ is bounded below, there exists a real number $\alpha$ such that $\alpha \leq s$ for all $s \in S$. Then, for any $t \in T$, we have $t = -s$ for some $s \in S$. Thus, we have \[ t = -s \leq -\alpha. \] Therefore, $T$ is bounded above by $-\alpha$.
Since $T$ is bounded above, it has a supremum. Let $\beta = \sup T$. We claim that $\inf S = -\beta$. To see this, first note that for any $s \in S$, we have $-s \in T$. Thus, we have \[ -s \leq \beta \implies s \geq -\beta. \] Therefore, $-\beta$ is a lower bound of $S$.
Next, let $\gamma$ be any lower bound of $S$. Then, we have $\gamma \leq s$ for all $s \in S$. Thus, for any $t \in T$, we have $t = -s$ for some $s \in S$. Therefore, we have \[ -t = s \geq \gamma \implies t \leq -\gamma. \] Thus, $-\gamma$ is an upper bound of $T$. Since $\beta = \sup T$, we have $\beta \leq -\gamma$. Therefore, we have \[ -\beta \geq \gamma. \] Since $\gamma$ is an arbitrary lower bound of $S$, we have shown that $-\beta$ is the greatest lower bound of $S$. Therefore, we have \[ \inf S = -\beta = -\sup T = -\sup \{-s: s \in S \}. \]
